title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Ngā Haerenga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship in Action: The Journey of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant local waterway that flows through Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. The stream's name references the native tree (mangakōtukutuku - tree fuchsia) and its journey from the Ruahine Ranges to the sea represents the interconnectedness of our environment, history, and community wellbeing.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Māori practices of water guardianship used by Ngāti Kahungunu along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Analyze current environmental data to assess the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and identify two key challenges affecting the waterway
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan that integrates mātauranga Māori with contemporary environmental management approaches
Collaborate with local kaitiaki to document oral histories and traditional knowledge related to the stream's significance

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Understanding Our Connection
activities.description: Students begin with a guided walk along sections of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, mapping significant sites and learning the traditional stories and place names. They create annotated maps showing both geographical features and cultural significance, using traditional Ngāti Kahungunu navigation markers and modern GPS technology.

activities.title: Wai Ora, Wai Māori: Water Quality Investigation
activities.description: Working with local environmental scientists, students conduct water testing at three points along the stream (source, mid-point, and confluence). They measure pH, clarity, nitrate levels, and macroinvertebrate diversity, comparing results with historical data and traditional indicators of water health used by Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: Kōrero with Kaitiaki: Learning from Local Guardians
activities.description: Students interview local kaumātua and environmental kaitiaki about traditional practices for protecting the awa. They document oral histories, traditional ecological knowledge, and learn specific protocols for interacting with and protecting the waterway according to Ngāti Kahungunu tikanga.

activities.title: Rāhui and Restoration: Developing Action Plans
activities.description: Based on their research, student groups develop practical kaitiakitanga action plans addressing identified issues. Plans must integrate both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches, including proposals for native planting, waste reduction initiatives, or community education programs specific to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans and historical records
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring data and GIS mapping tools
Local kaitiaki and kaumātua from Mangakōtukutuku area available for interviews and guidance
Digital recording equipment for documenting oral histories and environmental observations

tasks: Produce a detailed kaitiakitanga portfolio including: annotated maps, water quality analysis, interview transcripts, and reflective journal entries
Develop and present a comprehensive action plan for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku restoration
Create a digital story documenting the journey of learning and connection to the awa

nzcAlignment: Social Studies Level 5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Social Studies Level 5: Understand how people define and seek human rights
